1.synthesis: Moses and Aaron's first audience with Pharaoh begins with the full prophetic formula: 'Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel, "Let my people go, that they may hold a feast to me in the wilderness"' (v.1). Pharaoh's response cuts to the theological heart of the confrontation: 'Who is YHWH, that I should obey his voice and let Israel go? I do not know YHWH, and moreover, I will not let Israel go' (v.2). This is the central question the entire plague narrative will answer. Pharaoh's 'I do not know YHWH' is not ignorance but refusal: he is sovereign in Egypt, and no foreign deity's demand has force over him. His response is punitive: the Israelites are idle, he increases the quota — straw to be gathered by the workers themselves, while the brick production target remains unchanged (vv.6–9). The first confrontation with Pharaoh makes things worse before they get better.
1.historical_context: The prophetic messenger formula 'thus says YHWH' (koh ʾāmar YHWH) positions Moses and Aaron as diplomatic envoys of a superior king sending commands to a subordinate. Pharaoh's 'who is YHWH?' is the classic response of an imperial power to an unknown provincial deity's claim. Egyptian Pharaohs were themselves considered divine — the embodiment of Horus in life and Osiris in death. The demand to serve YHWH is thus a direct challenge to Pharaoh's own divine status. The brick-without-straw intensification has Egyptian administrative precedent: papyri document quotas and the monitoring of worker output. The increase in workload is designed to keep the Israelites too exhausted to organize or hope — the classic tool of oppressive labor management. The 'three-day journey to sacrifice' request was a minimal ask; Pharaoh treats even that as evidence of dangerous idleness.
1.literary_structure: The chapter's structure is a descent from initial confrontation (vv.1–5) through escalating oppression (vv.6–14) to the complaint of the Israelite foremen to Pharaoh (vv.15–19) and finally to Moses and Aaron's complaint to God (vv.20–23). Each stage is worse than the previous: Pharaoh rejects the demand, increases the workload, the foremen are beaten, the foremen blame Moses, and Moses accuses God. The chapter functions as the theological testing ground: things will get worse before the Exodus, and the faith expressed in 4:31 will crack under pressure. The question Pharaoh asks — 'who is YHWH?' — is the question the rest of Exodus answers through judgment and deliverance.
1.theological_application: Gill on Pharaoh's 'I know not the LORD': 'this was not owing to want of intelligence, but to pride and haughtiness of spirit … he refused to own him as Lord over him.' Matthew Henry: 'Pharaoh sets himself against God, and sets his own will and power in opposition to the will and power of God. He questions whether God has a right to give such a command; he denies that he himself is under any obligation to obey it.' The pattern recurs throughout history: the confrontation between divine authority and human power that refuses to yield. Pharaoh's hardness is not sudden but progressive — each plague encounter will deepen the confrontation until the tenth plague breaks the empire. 'Who is YHWH?' is the question every person faces; the answer comes through the Exodus.
1.christology: Pharaoh's 'I do not know YHWH' is the opposite of the Philippians 2 confession: 'every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father' (Philippians 2:11). The plagues that follow are YHWH's self-introduction to Egypt — 'by this you shall know that I am YHWH' (Exodus 7:17) — and ultimately all the earth will know. Christ's mission is the ultimate fulfilment of the Exodus self-revelation: the 'glory of YHWH revealed' that Isaiah promises (Isaiah 40:5) is the same glory that John says 'we have seen … the glory of the only Son from the Father' (John 1:14). The one who asks 'who is YHWH?' will receive the full answer in the cross and resurrection.

10.synthesis: The taskmasters implement Pharaoh's decree: gather your own straw but produce the same number of bricks (vv.10–14). The Israelite foremen, caught between Pharaoh's impossible demand and their people's inability, are beaten. They appeal to Pharaoh directly (vv.15–16), who dismisses them as idle. They leave the palace and encounter Moses and Aaron, and the foremen's judgment is direct: 'You have made us stink in the sight of Pharaoh and his servants, and have put a sword in their hand to kill us' (v.21). Moses, shaken, turns to YHWH with a lament that verges on accusation: 'O Lord, why have you done evil to this people? Why did you ever send me? For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has done evil to this people, and you have not delivered your people at all' (vv.22–23). The Exodus begins in apparent failure — before any plague, the deliverer complains that deliverance seems impossible.
10.historical_context: The 'straw' required for Egyptian brick-making served as a binding agent — chopped straw mixed into mud-brick prevented cracking. Brickfields without straw is not merely inconvenient but technically impossible at the required quality: the foremen are being set up to fail. The beating of the Israelite foremen (v.14) — Egyptian taskmasters using Israelite supervisors as intermediate agents, then punishing those supervisors for their people's failure — is the classic structure of colonial oppression: natives made complicit in their own oppression, then blamed for its failures. Moses's lament in vv.22–23 is the first of the great prophetic complaints: Jeremiah, the Psalmists, and Job will all use the same pattern of turning the complaint of apparent abandonment directly to God. The lament is itself an act of faith — Moses brings the crisis to God rather than abandoning the mission.
10.literary_structure: The chapter's descent structure (confrontation → worsening → betrayal → lament) sets up the divine response of ch.6. God will answer Moses's lament with the greatest covenant self-disclosure in the OT so far — the seven-fold promise of Exodus 6:6–8. The structural function of ch.5 is to strip away every human resource before God acts: the Israelites are more oppressed than before Moses arrived, the foremen oppose Moses, and Moses himself is in crisis. The 'worse before better' pattern is a recurring Exodus structure (each plague escalates, the Passover night is the darkest before the dawn). Providence often allows the situation to deteriorate fully before the deliverance comes — so that there is no human explanation for what follows.
10.theological_application: Gill on Moses's complaint: 'this he said not through impatience, or want of faith, or diffidence of God; but as an humble expostulation with God, expressing his grief and concern for the people, and his surprise at the divine conduct.' Matthew Henry: 'Moses's pious expostulation with God for the present suffering of Israel … He does not find fault with God, though he represents his own case, and the case of Israel, as very deplorable. He does not think of retreating from his business; he spreads his trouble before God.' The lament in vv.22–23 models authentic faith under pressure: it brings complaint to God rather than withdrawing from God. The valley of apparent divine absence is entered in prayer, not left in abandonment.
10.christology: The Israelite foremen's cry from their beaten-down position — and Moses's subsequent lament — enters the long tradition of corporate and prophetic complaint that reaches its apex in Gethsemane and the cross: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matthew 27:46). The deliverer who laments that deliverance is not coming mirrors Christ who, bearing the world's oppression, cries out from the darkness before resurrection. The 'bricks without straw' situation — impossible demands, no resources, increasing punishment — is the condition from which the true Exodus must come: deliverance that is entirely beyond human capacity, so that 'the surpassing power belongs to God and not to us' (2 Corinthians 4:7).
